Chief Complaint:
LEFT PLANTAR FOOT MELANOMA
 
Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure:
WIDE LOCAL EXCISION LEFT PLANTAR FOOT; VAC PLACEMENT

 
History of Present Illness:
This was an ___ man who recently had presented to an 
outside dermatologist with a draining lesion of the distal left 
plantar foot arising within a large heart shaped area of 
irregular pigmentation. A punch biopsy demonstrated an invasive 
acral lentiginous melanoma presented as fragments.  No formal 
depth could be given, although it seemed to be at least 0.6 mm 
in depth with ulceration.  After some discussion and 
consultation with his cardiologist, it was thought safe to 
proceed with definitive excision and concurrent sentinel lymph 
node biopsy for his minimal T1b lesion.

 
Past Medical History:
PMH: aortic stenosis, hypertension, obesity, early type 2 
diabetes mellitus, hyperlipidemia, glaucoma, and hardness of 
hearing.  He had a colonoscopy in year ___ which apparently 
showed a polyp but he has refused further studies.

PSH: open prostatectomy for BPH in the ___ in ___.  He 
underwent appendectomy, cataract extraction, and nonoperative 
management of a right ankle fracture.

 
Family History:
The family history is not significant for cancer or heart 
disease.  He has 2 daughters who are well.  ___ has twins, a 
___ boy and ___ girl, both ___ year students 
at ___ majoring in ___ and
pre-medicine.

 
Physical Exam:
General: AxOx3. Appears well
HEENT: Eyes anicteric. PEERLA. EOMI. Mucus membranes appear 
moist. 
Chest: Symmetric. CTAB. 
Cards: RRR. Nl S1/S2. No M/R/G.
Abdomen: nondistended, nontender
Neuro: Moving all extremities equally. Sensation grossly intact.
 
Pertinent Results:
___ 10:00PM BLOOD Glucose-91 UreaN-18 Creat-0.7 Na-141 
K-4.3 Cl-105 HCO3-27 AnGap-9*
___ 10:00PM BLOOD Calcium-8.3* Phos-3.8 Mg-2.___ M with ulcerated invasive melanoma of the left forefoot, at 
least T1b s/p WLE with VAC placement. He tolerated the procedure 
well without complications (Please see operative note for 
further details). After a brief and uneventful stay in the PACU, 
the patient was transferred to the floor for further 
post-operative management.

Neuro: The patient pain was well controlled on oral pain 
medication.
CV: The patient postoperatively noted to have bradycardia HR 
40-50. He was asymptomatic, VS routinely monitored. 
Pulm: The patient remained stable from a pulmonary standpoint; 
oxygen saturation was routinely monitored. Had good pulmonary 
toileting, as early ambulation and incentive spirometry were 
encouraged throughout hospitalization.

```json
  "diagnosis": "Acral lentiginous melanoma, left plantar foot, at least T1b (depth ≥0.6 mm with ulceration)",
  "biomarkers": [],
  "treatments": ["Wide local excision (WLE) left plantar foot", "Vacuum-assisted closure (VAC) placement", "Sentinel lymph node biopsy (planned/performed)"]
}
```

**Clinical Summary:**
This is a male patient with a significant past medical history including aortic stenosis, hypertension, obesity, and early type 2 diabetes mellitus, who was admitted for surgical management of a newly diagnosed left plantar foot melanoma. The lesion, described as a draining lesion within an area of irregular pigmentation, was biopsied and found to be an invasive acral lentiginous melanoma with a depth of at least 0.6 mm and ulceration, staging it as at least T1b. Following multidisciplinary consultation, particularly regarding cardiac risk, he underwent definitive wide local excision of the lesion with concurrent sentinel lymph node biopsy and placement of a vacuum-assisted closure (VAC) device. He tolerated the procedure well.

Postoperatively, his course was notable for asymptomatic bradycardia (HR 40-50), which was monitored closely. His pain was well-controlled with oral medications, and he remained stable from pulmonary and neurological standpoints, participating in early ambulation and incentive spirometry. Pertinent laboratory results were largely within normal limits. The patient was transferred to the floor for ongoing postoperative care and monitoring. The final pathology and sentinel lymph node results are pending and will guide further staging and management.
group_a.treatment_recommendations:
**Clinical Summary:** This is a male patient with a significant past medical history including aortic stenosis, hypertension, obesity, and early type 2 diabetes mellitus, who was admitted for surgical management of a newly diagnosed left plantar foot melanoma. The lesion, described as a draining lesion within an area of irregular pigmentation, was biopsied and found to be an invasive acral lentiginous melanoma with a depth of at least 0.6 mm and ulceration, staging it as at least T1b. Following multidisciplinary consultation, particularly regarding cardiac risk, he underwent definitive wide local excision of the lesion with concurrent sentinel lymph node biopsy and placement of a vacuum-assisted closure (VAC) device. He tolerated the procedure well. Postoperatively, his course was notable for asymptomatic bradycardia (HR 40-50), which was monitored closely. His pain was well-controlled with oral medications, and he remained stable from pulmonary and neurological standpoints, participating in early ambulation and incentive spirometry. Pertinent laboratory results were largely within normal limits. The patient was transferred to the floor for ongoing postoperative care and monitoring. The final pathology and sentinel lymph node results are pending and will guide further staging and management.

**Guideline Context:** NCCN 2026 Oncology Guidelines

**Treatment Plan:**
**1. Immediate Postoperative Management:**
*   Continue monitoring for postoperative bradycardia, given history of aortic stenosis. Cardiology consultation may be considered if bradycardia persists or becomes symptomatic.
*   Manage VAC dressing per standard protocol and monitor the surgical site for signs of infection or compromised healing, particularly given comorbidities (diabetes, obesity).
*   Continue multimodal pain management to facilitate early ambulation and pulmonary toileting.

**2. Pathologic Staging & Risk Assessment (Pending Results):**
*   **Wide Local Excision (WLE) Specimen:** Await final pathology report to confirm Breslow depth, ulceration status, mitotic rate, peripheral and deep margin status, and presence of lymphovascular invasion.
*   **Sentinel Lymph Node Biopsy (SLNB):** Await results. SLNB is indicated per NCCN guidelines for T1b tumors (≥0.8 mm with ulceration OR ≥0.8 mm without ulceration but with a mitotic rate ≥2/mm²). The biopsy depth of "at least 0.6 mm" necessitates final measurement.

**3. Definitive Surgical Management (Based on Final Pathology):**
*   **If margins are inadequate:** Re-excision to achieve recommended margins (1 cm for T1, 1-2 cm for T2, 2 cm for T3-T4).
*   **If SLNB is negative (pN0):** Complete wide local excision (if not already done with adequate margins). No further lymph node surgery is required. The patient enters surveillance.
*   **If SLNB is positive (pN1a or greater):**
    *   Complete lymph node dissection (CLND) of the involved nodal basin is an option, though may be omitted in select cases per NCCN. Discussion in a multidisciplinary tumor board is recommended.
    *   Staging advances to Stage III.

**4. Adjuvant Systemic Therapy & Staging (If indicated):**
*   **For Stage IIB/C (high-risk primary, SLNB negative):** Consider adjuvant therapy options (e.g., immunotherapy, targeted therapy if *BRAF* V600 mutation positive) based on final risk features. Discuss benefits/risks with patient.
*   **For Stage III (SLNB positive):** Strongly consider adjuvant systemic therapy (immunotherapy or targeted therapy) to reduce risk of recurrence. Full staging with imaging (CT CAP +/- brain MRI/PET-CT) is recommended prior to initiating therapy.

**5. Long-term Surveillance & Management:**
*   **Skin Exam:** History and physical exam with full skin and nodal assessment every 3-12 months based on final stage.
*   **Imaging:** For Stage IIB/C and III, consider cross-sectional imaging (CT, MRI, or PET/CT) every 6-12 months for the first few years.
*   **Comorbidity Management:** Coordinate care with primary care/cardiology for optimization of aortic stenosis, hypertension, diabetes, and hyperlipidemia.
*   **Genetic Counseling:** Consider germline genetic testing given the diagnosis of acral lentiginous melanoma.
*   **Patient Education:** Sun protection, self-skin exams, and signs/symptoms of recurrence.

**Clinical Summary:**  
This is a male patient with a significant past medical history including aortic stenosis, hypertension, obesity, and early type 2 diabetes mellitus, who presented with a draining lesion on his distal left plantar foot. A prior punch biopsy confirmed the diagnosis of invasive acral lentiginous melanoma, with a depth of at least 0.6 mm and ulceration, staging it as at least a T1b lesion. After medical clearance, he underwent definitive surgical management with a wide local excision of the left plantar foot melanoma, concurrent sentinel lymph node biopsy, and placement of a vacuum-assisted closure (VAC) device. Post-operatively, he was hemodynamically stable with well-controlled pain. He was noted to have asymptomatic bradycardia (HR 40-50), which was monitored. Pertinent molecular testing via Pathology AI and OncoKB revealed no actionable solid tumor biomarkers or genomic targets for this melanoma. His post-operative course on the floor was otherwise uncomplicated, with encouragement of early ambulation and pulmonary hygiene.
group_b.treatment_recommendations:
**Clinical Summary:** This is a male patient with a significant past medical history including aortic stenosis, hypertension, obesity, and early type 2 diabetes mellitus, who presented with a draining lesion on his distal left plantar foot. A prior punch biopsy confirmed the diagnosis of invasive acral lentiginous melanoma, with a depth of at least 0.6 mm and ulceration, staging it as at least a T1b lesion. After medical clearance, he underwent definitive surgical management with a wide local excision of the left plantar foot melanoma, concurrent sentinel lymph node biopsy, and placement of a vacuum-assisted closure (VAC) device. Post-operatively, he was hemodynamically stable with well-controlled pain. He was noted to have asymptomatic bradycardia (HR 40-50), which was monitored. Pertinent molecular testing via Pathology AI and OncoKB revealed no actionable solid tumor biomarkers or genomic targets for this melanoma. His post-operative course on the floor was otherwise uncomplicated, with encouragement of early ambulation and pulmonary hygiene.

**Guideline Context:** NCCN 2026 Oncology Guidelines

**Treatment Plan:**
**1.  Post-operative Wound Care:**
    *   Continue management of the VAC device per standard protocol until adequate wound healing or transition to standard dressing is appropriate.
    *   Monitor for signs of infection, dehiscence, or impaired healing, particularly given the location (plantar foot) and patient comorbidities (diabetes).

**2.  Sentinel Lymph Node Biopsy (SLNB) Results & Staging Completion:**
    *   **Critical Next Step:** Await final pathology from the wide local excision (including definitive Breslow depth, ulceration status, mitotic rate) and the sentinel lymph node biopsy.
    *   **Staging:** Final AJCC staging (pTNM) will be determined based on these results. For a T1b lesion, SLNB is guideline-concordant.

**3.  Adjuvant Therapy Consideration (Pending Final Pathology):**
    *   If the SLNB is negative (Stage I/II), no adjuvant systemic therapy is recommended. Follow-up will consist of surveillance per NCCN guidelines.
    *   If the SLNB is positive (Stage III), adjuvant therapy options should be discussed. Given the biomarker testing shows no actionable targets for targeted therapy (e.g., BRAF/MEK inhibitors), the primary adjuvant options per NCCN would be:
        *   **Immunotherapy:** Consider adjuvant nivolumab +/- relatlimab or pembrolizumab, based on the patient's overall health status, comorbidities (especially cardiac history), and performance status.
    *   A formal oncology consultation is recommended to review final pathology, discuss risks/benefits of adjuvant therapy, and coordinate care.

**4.  Surveillance & Follow-up:**
    *   Schedule follow-up with surgical oncology and/or dermatology for wound check and suture removal as indicated.
    *   Establish a long-term surveillance plan based on final stage, including:
        *   Regular skin and nodal basin exams.
        *   Consideration of imaging (e.g., CT, PET/CT, or MRI) for higher-risk stages.
        *   Patient education on skin self-exams and sun protection.

**5.  Comorbidity & Risk Factor Management:**
    *   Given history of aortic stenosis and post-operative bradycardia, coordinate care with cardiology for ongoing cardiac assessment.
    *   Optimize management of diabetes, hypertension, and hyperlipidemia to support healing and reduce overall cardiovascular risk.
    *   Encourage weight management and smoking cessation if applicable.

**Clinical Summary:**  
This is a male patient with a significant past medical history including aortic stenosis, hypertension, obesity, early type 2 diabetes mellitus, hyperlipidemia, glaucoma, and hearing loss. He presented with a draining lesion on his left plantar foot, which was biopsied and diagnosed as an invasive acral lentiginous melanoma. The lesion was at least 0.6 mm deep with ulceration, classifying it as at least a T1b melanoma. After cardiology clearance, he underwent definitive surgical management with a wide local excision of the left plantar foot melanoma, concurrent sentinel lymph node biopsy, and placement of a vacuum-assisted closure (VAC) device. His post-surgical status is currently stable.
group_c.treatment_recommendations:
**Clinical Summary:** This is a male patient with a significant past medical history including aortic stenosis, hypertension, obesity, early type 2 diabetes mellitus, hyperlipidemia, glaucoma, and hearing loss. He presented with a draining lesion on his left plantar foot, which was biopsied and diagnosed as an invasive acral lentiginous melanoma. The lesion was at least 0.6 mm deep with ulceration, classifying it as at least a T1b melanoma. After cardiology clearance, he underwent definitive surgical management with a wide local excision of the left plantar foot melanoma, concurrent sentinel lymph node biopsy, and placement of a vacuum-assisted closure (VAC) device. Postoperatively, he was stable with well-controlled pain. A notable finding was asymptomatic bradycardia (heart rate 40-50), which was monitored closely. He otherwise had an uncomplicated postoperative course with early ambulation and incentive spirometry. Tumor biomarker and genomic analyses did not reveal any currently actionable targets. His post-surgical status is currently stable.

**Guideline Context:** NCCN 2026 Oncology Guidelines

**Treatment Plan:**

**1. Postoperative Management & Surveillance:**
*   **Pathology Review:** Await final pathology from the wide local excision and sentinel lymph node biopsy (SLNB). The definitive pathologic stage (pT category, ulceration status, mitotic rate, SLNB status) will guide subsequent management.
*   **Wound Care:** Continue management of the VAC device per surgical team's protocol until the wound is ready for closure or grafting.
*   **Cardiac Monitoring:** Continue monitoring for asymptomatic bradycardia. Given history of aortic stenosis, coordinate with cardiology for follow-up as needed.
*   **Comorbidity Management:** Optimize management of hypertension, diabetes, and hyperlipidemia in collaboration with primary care.

**2. Adjuvant Therapy Considerations (Pending Final Pathology):**
*   Per NCCN guidelines, adjuvant therapy recommendations are primarily based on final pathologic staging (AJCC 9th Edition).
*   **If SLNB is negative (Stage I/II):** No adjuvant systemic therapy is recommended. Enter into surveillance protocol.
*   **If SLNB is positive (Stage III):**
    *   Discuss adjuvant systemic therapy options, which may include immunotherapy (e.g., pembrolizumab, nivolumab) or targeted therapy (if *BRAF V600E/K* mutation is present).
    *   Given the patient's multiple comorbidities (especially aortic stenosis), a thorough risk-benefit assessment and potential cardiology re-evaluation are required prior to initiating any systemic therapy.

**3. Surveillance Protocol (Per NCCN Guidelines):**
*   **History & Physical Exam:** Every 3-6 months for 2 years, then every 6-12 months for years 3-5, then annually thereafter.
*   **Skin & Lymph Node Exam:** At each visit.
*   **Imaging:** Not routinely recommended for Stage I/II disease. For Stage III disease or higher, consider imaging (CT, PET/CT, or MRI) every 6-12 months for 3 years, then as clinically indicated.
*   **Patient Education:** Instruct on monthly self-skin exams and sun protection.

**4. Genetic Counseling (Consider):** While acral lentiginous melanoma is less frequently associated with germline mutations than other subtypes, consider referral for genetic counseling based on personal history (multiple primary melanomas) or strong family history (if present).

**5. Follow-up:**
*   Schedule follow-up appointment with surgical oncology for wound check and review of final pathology in 1-2 weeks.
*   Coordinate ongoing care with dermatology for full-body skin exams.
*   Ensure primary care follow-up for management of chronic medical conditions.

The initial punch biopsy indicated a depth of at least 0.6 mm with ulceration, classifying it as a clinical T1b lesion. He has undergone a wide local excision with sentinel lymph node biopsy (SLNB). The critical pending result is the SLNB pathology, which will definitively stage the disease and dictate all subsequent management.

**Scored Guidelines:** [Notice] No highly relevant external guidelines found. Rely on latest internal NCCN oncology consensus to generate specific drug recommendations.

The presentation is complicated by symptoms and signs of volume depletion, including orthostatic hypotension and tachycardia noted at her PCP's office, and an elevated serum creatinine from her baseline. Evaluation in the emergency department revealed a low-grade inflammatory state with an elevated CRP, a stable but notable anemia, and a "dirty" urinalysis suggestive of a concurrent urinary tract infection contributing to her dysuria. Abdominal imaging was unremarkable for an acute surgical process. Initial management focused on volume resuscitation with intravenous fluids, empiric antibiotic coverage for the suspected UTI, and electrolyte repletion. She was admitted to the medicine service for further management of her multifactorial clinical picture, which includes acute-on-chronic kidney injury, dehydration, a suspected UTI, and a likely flare of her underlying gastroduodenitis driving her oral intolerance and weight loss.
group_a.treatment_recommendations:
Clinical Summary: This is an elderly female with a complex past medical history, including diastolic heart failure, chronic kidney disease, and a known history of gastritis/duodenitis, who presents with a primary complaint of poor oral intake, early satiety, and significant weight loss of 30 pounds over three months. The presentation is complicated by symptoms and signs of volume depletion, including orthostatic hypotension and tachycardia noted at her PCP's office, and an acute-on-chronic kidney injury. Evaluation revealed a low-grade inflammatory state with an elevated CRP, a stable but notable anemia, and a "dirty" urinalysis suggestive of a concurrent urinary tract infection. Abdominal imaging was unremarkable for an acute surgical process. Initial management focused on volume resuscitation, empiric antibiotics for a suspected UTI, and electrolyte repletion.

Guideline Context: NCCN 2026 Oncology Guidelines
Treatment Plan: **Note: The NCCN Oncology Guidelines are not directly applicable to this patient's primary admission diagnoses, which are non-oncologic (dehydration, AKI on CKD, UTI, gastritis). However, the patient's significant, unintentional weight loss (>5% in 3 months) and anorexia meet criteria for a cancer screening workup per NCCN guidelines for cancer-associated weight loss and anorexia. The treatment plan below addresses the acute issues and incorporates guideline-concordant evaluation for the underlying cause of weight loss.**

**1. Acute Stabilization & Management:**
*   **Volume & Electrolyte Repletion:** Continue IV isotonic fluids with careful monitoring of volume status given history of diastolic CHF and CKD. Replete electrolytes as needed.
*   **Infection:** Complete a course of antibiotics for urinary tract infection, tailored to culture results. Monitor for resolution of dysuria and systemic inflammation.
*   **Nutrition & Gastrointestinal Symptoms:**
    *   Initiate a proton pump inhibitor (PPI) for suspected gastritis/duodenitis flare.
    *   Consider antiemetics (e.g., ondansetron) for nausea, avoiding medications with anticholinergic effects given her age.
    *   Consult Clinical Nutrition/Dietary for a comprehensive assessment. Given profound anorexia and weight loss, consider a trial of a pharmacologic appetite stimulant such as megestrol acetate or dronabinol, following a review of risks/benefits (e.g., fluid retention, dizziness, thrombotic risk).
*   **Renal & Cardiac:** Monitor renal function closely. Adjust all medications for CKD. Manage blood pressure and volume cautiously to support renal perfusion without precipitating pulmonary edema.

**2. Evaluation for Underlying Etiology of Weight Loss & Anorexia (Per NCCN Principles):**
*   **Diagnostic Workup:** Conduct a focused evaluation for occult malignancy or other serious pathology, as the weight loss is severe and unexplained.
    *   **Imaging:** Consider contrast-enhanced CT of the chest/abdomen/pelvis (if renal function permits) to evaluate for malignancy, given history of mediastinal mass. A low-dose non-contrast CT may be an alternative.
    *   **Endoscopy:** Given persistent upper GI symptoms and history of gastritis, esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGD) should be strongly considered during this admission to rule out malignant or peptic ulcer disease.
    *   **Laboratory:** Check additional tumor markers if clinically indicated (e.g., CEA, CA 19-9) based on risk factors and symptoms. Check TSH to rule out hyperthyroidism.
*   **Multidisciplinary Support:** Referral to a **Registered Dietitian (RD)** for personalized nutritional intervention and to **Physical Therapy** to address deconditioning and knee pain is essential.

**3. Goals of Care & Discharge Planning:**
*   Engage the patient and family in a discussion about goals of care, values, and preferences, especially regarding the intensity of a diagnostic workup for weight loss.
*   Plan for close outpatient follow-up with Primary Care and relevant specialists (Gastroenterology, Nephrology, Cardiology).
*   Arrange for home health services or additional support to ensure nutritional and functional recovery post-discharge.

group_b.treatment_recommendations:
Clinical Summary: This is an elderly female with a complex past medical history, including diastolic heart failure, chronic kidney disease, and a history of gastritis/duodenitis, who presents with a primary complaint of persistent poor oral intake, early satiety, nausea, and significant weight loss of 30 pounds over three months. She was found to be orthostatic at her PCP's office, corroborating volume depletion. Evaluation in the emergency department revealed a urinary tract infection, treated with ceftriaxone, and acute kidney injury with a creatinine rise to 1.6 from a baseline of 0.8-1.0. She received aggressive fluid resuscitation, magnesium repletion, and symptomatic management. A CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis showed no acute surgical process. The leading etiology for her presentation is a flare of her known gastritis/duodenitis, compounded by volume depletion and a superimposed infection. She was admitted to the medicine service for continued management of her oral intolerance, volume status, acute kidney injury, and urinary tract infection.

Guideline Context: NCCN 2026 Oncology Guidelines

Treatment Plan:
**1. Nutritional & Symptom Management (Aligned with NCCN Guidelines for Supportive Care):**
*   **Nutritional Assessment & Support:** Formal assessment by a registered dietitian. Given significant weight loss (>5% in 3 months) and persistent symptoms, initiate oral nutritional supplements. If oral intake remains inadequate, consider a short-term trial of pharmacologic appetite stimulants (e.g., megestrol acetate or dronabinol), with careful consideration of side effect profiles (fluid retention, delirium) given her comorbidities.
*   **Antiemetic Regimen:** Continue and optimize scheduled antiemetic therapy for chronic nausea. A trial of a prokinetic agent (e.g., metoclopramide) may be considered for early satiety, pending resolution of acute kidney injury and with monitoring for extrapyramidal side effects.
*   **Pain Management:** Continue acetaminophen for knee pain. Avoid NSAIDs (ibuprofen allergy and CKD history). For abdominal pain, evaluate for and treat gastritis specifically.

**2. Manage Acute Medical Issues:**
*   **Volume & AKI Management:** Continue IV fluid resuscitation with balanced crystalloids to correct orthostasis and prerenal AKI, with careful monitoring for volume overload given diastolic heart failure. Monitor daily weights, strict I/O, and trend creatinine.
*   **Infection Management:** Complete a standard course of antibiotics for the diagnosed urinary tract infection, with antibiotic selection adjusted for CKD stage.

**3. Diagnostic Evaluation (to rule out occult malignancy as per NCCN principles for unexplained weight loss):**
*   **Age-Appropriate Cancer Screening:** Ensure she is up-to-date with colorectal cancer screening (given diverticulosis history) and breast cancer screening.
*   **Evaluate Unexplained Weight Loss:** Given the significant, unintentional weight loss and history of a mediastinal mass, consider:
    *   **Upper Endoscopy (EGD):** To directly evaluate and biopsy the gastritis/duodenitis, ruling out malignant or infectious (e.g., H. pylori) etiology.
    *   **Tumor Markers/Imaging:** While the CT A/P was negative, a more focused evaluation for her mediastinal mass history may be warranted if symptoms persist. A low threshold to review prior imaging and consider a follow-up CT chest if clinically indicated.

**4. Comorbidity Optimization:**
*   **Heart Failure:** Manage fluid balance meticulously. Continue guideline-directed medical therapy as tolerated, avoiding hypotension.
*   **Chronic Kidney Disease:** Avoid nephrotoxins. Adjust all medications for renal function.
*   **Diabetes:** Monitor blood glucose closely, especially with changes in nutritional intake and steroid use if initiated.

**5. Multidisciplinary Planning:**
*   Coordinate with dietary, pharmacy, and possibly gastroenterology for endoscopic evaluation.
*   Assess discharge needs, including potential home health for nutritional support and medication management.

group_c.treatment_recommendations:
Clinical Summary: An elderly female with a history of diastolic heart failure, chronic kidney disease (stage III), and gastritis/duodenitis presents with a 3-month history of progressive oral intolerance, early satiety, and a 30-pound weight loss. Associated symptoms include nausea, abdominal pain, orthostatic lightheadedness, and dysuria. Key findings include orthostatic hypotension, Hgb 8.7, CRP 54.6, creatinine 1.6 (baseline ~1.0), and a "dirty" urinalysis. She was treated in the ED with IV fluids, ceftriaxone for a suspected UTI, and supportive medications. CT A/P showed no acute process. She is admitted for management of multifactorial decline, including failure to thrive, AKI on CKD, infection, and orthostasis.

Guideline Context: NCCN 2026 Oncology Guidelines

Treatment Plan:
**1. Primary Problem Management: Failure to Thrive / Cachexia Syndrome (R63.4)**
*   **Nutritional Assessment & Support:** Formal consultation with Clinical Dietitian/Nutritionist. Initiate high-calorie, high-protein oral nutritional supplements. Consider appetite stimulant per NCCN guidelines (e.g., megestrol acetate or dronabinol), with careful monitoring for fluid retention (given CHF) and delirium. **Contraindicated:** Corticosteroids due to history of T2DM.
*   **Symptom Management:** Continue scheduled antiemetics (e.g., ondansetron) for nausea. **Avoid:** NSAIDs (ibuprofen allergy) and opioid analgesics (codeine/morphine allergy) for pain management. Utilize acetaminophen and non-pharmacologic measures.
*   **Etiologic Work-up:** Given significant weight loss and elevated CRP, expedite evaluation for occult malignancy as a contributing factor, despite history of benign mediastinal mass. Consider age-appropriate cancer screening if not current and evaluate for new primary.

**2. Acute Kidney Injury on Chronic Kidney Disease (N17.9, N18.3)**
*   **Monitoring:** Strict I&O, daily weights, and trend serum creatinine/electrolytes.
*   **Management:** Ensure euvolemia with judicious IV fluids, balancing CHF risk. Discontinue any potential nephrotoxins. The ceftriaxone for UTI is appropriate; monitor levels if continued. Re-evaluate need for magnesium repletion given renal impairment.

**3. Urinary Tract Infection (N39.0)**
*   **Antibiotic Therapy:** Continue IV ceftriaxone 1g daily for uncomplicated UTI, given allergy to sulfa antibiotics. Transition to oral agent based on culture sensitivities and clinical improvement, selecting a non-sulfa alternative (e.g., nitrofurantoin if CrCl >30, or amoxicillin).
*   **Monitoring:** Follow-up urinalysis and culture.

**4. Orthostatic Hypotension (I95.1)**
*   **Non-pharmacologic:** Encourage increased oral fluid intake as tolerated, liberalize dietary salt if not contraindicated by CHF/HTN, and educate on gradual position changes. Consider compression stockings.
*   **Pharmacologic:** Hold any non-essential antihypertensives. If symptoms persist after volume repletion and treatment of infection, consider pharmacologic review and specialist consultation.

**5. Comorbidity Management**
*   **Diastolic CHF (I50.3):** Monitor for volume overload with daily weights and cardiopulmonary exam. Resume home regimen cautiously once AKI and orthostasis are improving.
*   **Pain Management (Left Knee):** Continue acetaminophen. **Avoid:** NSAIDs (allergy). Consider topical agents (e.g., lidocaine) or physical therapy evaluation.
*   **Allergies:** Document and adhere to allergy list. **Avoid all:** Quinine, sulfa antibiotics, codeine, morphine, ibuprofen, propranolol.

**6. Discharge Planning & Goals**
*   **Goals for Discharge:** Tolerating oral intake with stabilized weight, resolution of AKI (creatinine trending to baseline), completion of UTI treatment, and management of orthostatic symptoms.
*   **Planning:** Early involvement of Social Work and Case Management to assess home support needs and plan for safe discharge, possibly requiring rehabilitation given recent TKR and functional decline.

The immediate priority is diagnostic confirmation and comprehensive supportive care to stabilize her condition for potential anti-cancer therapy.

**Clinical Summary:**  
This is a previously healthy male who presents with a one-month history of constitutional and flu-like symptoms, culminating in 1.5 days of anuria prompting admission. Initial workup revealed acute renal failure, anemia, leukocytosis, and a retrocardiac density on chest imaging suggestive of pneumonia. His prolonged debilitation has resulted in a sacral decubitus ulcer, and he was found to have oral thrush on examination. In the emergency department, he was initiated on broad-spectrum antibiotics (vancomycin and zosyn, following prior levofloxacin) and received a blood transfusion for anemia. Given his anuric renal failure, he has undergone placement of a tunneled dialysis line and initiation of hemodialysis, with an A-V fistula placed for long-term access. The overall presentation is concerning for a severe systemic illness, potentially infectious or inflammatory, leading to acute kidney injury requiring renal replacement therapy, with additional complications of malnutrition, thrush, and a pressure injury.
group_a.treatment_recommendations:
Clinical Summary: A previously healthy male presents with a one-month history of constitutional symptoms (weight loss, night sweats, malaise) and a recent 1.5 days of anuria. Workup reveals acute renal failure requiring hemodialysis (via tunneled line, with AV fistula placed), anemia, leukocytosis, and a chest x-ray finding suggestive of pneumonia. Complications include oral thrush and a sacral decubitus ulcer. He was initiated on broad-spectrum antibiotics (levofloxacin, then vancomycin/zosyn) and received a PRBC transfusion. The overall presentation is concerning for a severe systemic illness of unclear etiology.

Guideline Context: NCCN 2026 Oncology Guidelines

Treatment Plan:
**Primary Objective:** Determine the underlying etiology of the systemic illness and acute kidney injury, with a high index of suspicion for a potential oncologic or paraneoplastic process given the presentation of constitutional symptoms, weight loss, anemia, and acute renal failure.
1.  **Diagnostic Workup:**
    *   **Oncology-Focused Evaluation:** Given the guideline context, an expedited workup for occult malignancy is warranted.
        *   **Imaging:** CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast (if renal function permits post-dialysis) to evaluate for lymphadenopathy, solid tumors, or retroperitoneal pathology.
        *   **Laboratory:** Serum and urine protein electrophoresis (SPEP/UPEP) with immunofixation, free light chains (kappa/lambda) with ratio to evaluate for plasma cell dyscrasias. LDH, uric acid. Consider tumor markers (e.g., PSA, AFP, beta-hCG) based on demographics and exam.
        *   **Tissue Diagnosis:** Review kidney biopsy pathology with hematopathology consultation to rule out lymphomatous or leukemic infiltration, cast nephropathy, or other paraneoplastic glomerulopathies.
    *   **Infectious Disease:** Continue broad-spectrum antibiotics for presumed pneumonia. Obtain blood cultures, fungal cultures, and consider infectious disease consultation given thrush and night sweats. Workup for endemic fungi, mycobacteria, and atypical infections should be considered.
    *   **Renal:** Continue hemodialysis as needed. Follow serial electrolytes, fluid status, and urine output.
    *   **Supportive Care:** Formal wound care consultation for sacral decubitus ulcer. Nutritional assessment and support. Antifungal treatment for oral thrush (e.g., nystatin swish and swallow or fluconazole).

2.  **Consultations:**
    *   Nephrology (already involved for dialysis).
    *   Hematology/Oncology for guidance on diagnostic evaluation for possible malignancy.
    *   Infectious Disease.
    *   Wound Care.
    *   Nutrition.

3.  **Monitoring:**
    *   Daily weights, strict I/O.
    *   Serial CBC, CMP, magnesium, phosphate.
    *   Monitor for signs of infection, tumor lysis syndrome (once etiology is identified), and dialysis access complications.

4.  **Patient Education:**
    *   Discuss the diagnostic plan, emphasizing the need to identify the root cause of his illness.
    *   Educate on dialysis process and access care.
    *   Reinforce importance of wound care and nutrition.

**Clinical Summary:**  
This is a previously healthy male who presents with acute anuria of 1.5 days duration, prompting hospitalization. His presentation is preceded by a one-month history of a systemic prodrome including flu-like symptoms, productive cough, significant weight loss, poor appetite, vomiting, and night sweats, complicated by prolonged bedrest resulting in a sacral decubitus ulcer. Initial evaluation revealed renal failure, anemia, leukocytosis, and a retrocardiac density on chest imaging. On admission, he was found to have oral thrush. He was initiated on broad-spectrum antimicrobial therapy with vancomycin and piperacillin-tazobactam and received a blood transfusion for anemia. Given the severity of his renal failure, he has required the placement of a tunneled dialysis line for hemodialysis and an arteriovenous fistula, with a kidney biopsy planned for definitive diagnosis. The overall clinical picture is concerning for a severe acute kidney injury in the setting of a possible underlying infectious, inflammatory, or systemic illness.
group_b.treatment_recommendations:
Clinical Summary: A previously healthy male presents with acute anuria and severe acute kidney injury requiring emergent hemodialysis, preceded by a one-month systemic prodrome (flu-like symptoms, weight loss, night sweats). Findings include oral thrush, a sacral decubitus ulcer, anemia, leukocytosis, and a chest imaging abnormality. He is on broad-spectrum antibiotics and has undergone dialysis access placement. A kidney biopsy is planned to determine the etiology, with a differential including severe infection, rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis, or a systemic inflammatory/oncologic process.

Guideline Context: NCCN 2026 Oncology Guidelines

Treatment Plan:
**1. Diagnostic & Staging Workup:**
*   **Priority:** Expedite **kidney biopsy** to establish histopathological diagnosis. Tissue should be sent for routine histology, immunofluorescence, and electron microscopy.
*   **Oncology-Focused Metastatic Workup:** Given constitutional symptoms (weight loss, night sweats) and acute renal failure of unknown etiology, initiate a workup for occult malignancy or paraneoplastic syndrome per NCCN principles for cancer of unknown primary (CUP).
    *   **Imaging:** **Contrast-enhanced CT of chest/abdomen/pelvis** (post-dialysis, if renal function permits) to identify solid tumors, lymphadenopathy, or retroperitoneal pathology. Consider **PET-CT** if CT is non-diagnostic.
    *   **Laboratory:** **Serum & urine protein electrophoresis (SPEP/UPEP) with immunofixation** to evaluate for monoclonal gammopathy (e.g., multiple myeloma). **Quantitative immunoglobulins**. **Tumor markers** (e.g., PSA, AFP, beta-hCG) as clinically indicated based on presentation and biopsy results.
    *   **Additional Studies:** **HIV test**, **ANA, ANCA, anti-GBM, anti-dsDNA, complement levels** to evaluate for autoimmune/vasculitic causes.

**2. Management of Acute Issues:**
*   **Renal:** Continue **intermittent hemodialysis** as needed. Manage electrolytes and volume status meticulously.
*   **Infectious Disease:**
    *   Continue broad-spectrum antibiotics (**vancomycin + piperacillin-tazobactam**) pending culture results and diagnostic clarity. Adjust dosing for renal replacement therapy.
    *   Treat **oral thrush** with topical (nystatin) or systemic (fluconazole) antifungal therapy.
    *   Manage **sacral decubitus ulcer** with standard wound care.
*   **Supportive Care:**
    *   **Nutrition:** Involve renal dietitian. Provide nutritional support given significant weight loss and catabolic state.
    *   **Anemia:** Monitor hemoglobin. Hold further transfusions unless symptomatic, pending diagnostic workup.
    *   **Thromboprophylaxis:** Consider pharmacologic VTE prophylaxis given prolonged immobility, if no contraindications.

**3. Definitive Treatment Pathway:**
*   Treatment will be **entirely directed by the kidney biopsy and oncology workup results**.
*   **If biopsy reveals a primary renal pathology** (e.g., crescentic GN), coordinate care with Nephrology for appropriate immunosuppression.
*   **If workup reveals an underlying malignancy,** treatment will follow the specific **NCCN Guidelines** for the diagnosed malignancy, coordinated with Medical Oncology. This may involve systemic therapy, radiation, or surgery, with close attention to renal dosing and supportive care during dialysis.
*   **If an infectious etiology is confirmed,** tailor antimicrobial therapy accordingly and discontinue unnecessary broad-spectrum agents.

**4. Follow-up & Monitoring:**
*   Multidisciplinary coordination between Nephrology, Oncology, Infectious Disease, and Primary Care.
*   Monitor renal function for recovery. Assess AV fistula maturity for potential transition from tunneled line.
*   Schedule follow-up based on final diagnosis to initiate and monitor definitive therapy per NCCN Guidelines.

**Clinical Summary:**  
This is a previously healthy male who presented with 1.5 days of anuria following a one-month prodrome of constitutional and flu-like symptoms, including productive cough, weight loss, poor appetite, vomiting, and night sweats. His presentation was marked by acute renal failure, anemia, and leukocytosis. Initial management in the emergency department included broad-spectrum antibiotics (vancomycin and piperacillin-tazobactam) for a suspected infection, as well as a blood transfusion for anemia. He was found to have oral thrush and a sacral decubitus ulcer secondary to prolonged bedrest. The acute kidney injury progressed to anuria requiring immediate renal replacement therapy, for which a tunneled dialysis catheter was placed. A kidney biopsy and creation of an arteriovenous fistula were performed for definitive diagnosis and long-term dialysis access, respectively. The overall clinical picture is concerning for a severe systemic illness—potentially infectious, inflammatory, or vasculitic in origin—leading to rapid renal failure. The workup is ongoing to determine the underlying etiology.
group_c.treatment_recommendations:
**Clinical Summary:** This is a previously healthy male who presented with 1.5 days of anuria following a one-month prodrome of constitutional and flu-like symptoms, including productive cough, weight loss, poor appetite, vomiting, and night sweats. His presentation was marked by acute renal failure, anemia, and leukocytosis. Initial management in the emergency department included broad-spectrum antibiotics (vancomycin and piperacillin-tazobactam) for a suspected infection, as well as a blood transfusion for anemia. He was found to have oral thrush and a sacral decubitus ulcer secondary to prolonged bedrest. The acute kidney injury progressed to anuria requiring immediate renal replacement therapy, for which a tunneled dialysis catheter was placed. A kidney biopsy and creation of an arteriovenous fistula were performed for definitive diagnosis and long-term dialysis access, respectively. The overall clinical picture is concerning for a severe systemic illness—potentially infectious, inflammatory, or vasculitic in origin—leading to rapid renal failure. The workup is ongoing to determine the underlying etiology.

**Guideline Context:** NCCN 2026 Oncology Guidelines

**Treatment Plan:**
**Note:** The patient's presentation is not consistent with a primary oncologic diagnosis. The NCCN guidelines are not directly applicable to the management of acute renal failure of unclear, potentially infectious or inflammatory etiology. The primary focus remains on supportive care and definitive diagnosis via renal biopsy. However, the NCCN guidelines for supportive care and management of complications in cancer patients can inform certain aspects of care.

1.  **Primary Diagnosis & Management:**
    *   **Renal Failure:** Continue hemodialysis as prescribed. Await histopathology results from kidney biopsy to guide definitive diagnosis (e.g., rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis, acute interstitial nephritis, vasculitis).
    *   **Infectious Workup & Management:**
        *   **Antibiotics:** Re-evaluate the need for broad-spectrum antibiotics (vancomycin/piperacillin-tazobactam) based on culture results (blood, urine, sputum if available) and clinical status. De-escalate or discontinue if no source is identified.
        *   **Oral Thrush:** Treat with topical antifungal agents (e.g., nystatin swish and swallow, clotrimazole troches) or systemic fluconazole if severe or refractory.
        *   **Sacral Decubitus Ulcer:** Implement standard wound care per institutional protocol (e.g., offloading, moisture management, appropriate dressings). Monitor for signs of infection.

2.  **Supportive Care (Informed by NCCN Principles):**
    *   **Anemia:** Monitor hemoglobin. Transfuse packed red blood cells per NCCN guidelines for cancer- and chemotherapy-associated anemia (e.g., threshold Hb < 7-8 g/dL in stable patients, or < 9 g/dL in symptomatic patients). Evaluate for other causes of anemia (e.g., iron deficiency, inflammation).
    *   **Nutrition:** Consult clinical nutrition. Address poor appetite and weight loss. Consider nutritional supplementation to meet increased metabolic demands and support wound healing.
    *   **Antiemetics:** Manage vomiting as needed. Per NCCN antiemesis guidelines, consider a serotonin (5-HT3) receptor antagonist (e.g., ondansetron) for moderate to high emetic risk, though the etiology here is not chemotherapy-related.
    *   **Thromboprophylaxis:** Given prolonged bed rest and acute illness, assess risk for venous thromboembolism (VTE). Consider pharmacologic VTE prophylaxis per NCCN guidelines for medically ill patients, barring contraindications from renal failure or bleeding risk.

3.  **Definitive Workup & Next Steps:**
    *   **Biopsy Results:** The kidney biopsy result is the critical next step. Based on findings, consultation with Nephrology and potentially Rheumatology (for vasculitis) or Infectious Disease will be paramount.
    *   **Imaging:** The "stable disease (SD)" per imaging assessment note is incongruent without a prior known malignancy. Clarify this finding. Consider repeat chest imaging to evaluate the "retrocardiac density" and for signs of systemic disease (e.g., pulmonary-renal syndrome).
    *   **Additional Labs:** Based on biopsy suspicion, send serologies for ANCA, anti-GBM, ANA, anti-dsDNA, complement levels, cryoglobulins, and infectious serologies (e.g., HIV, hepatitis B/C).

4.  **Patient Support:**
    *   Address the patient's expressed anxiety regarding hospitals with supportive counseling and clear communication.
    *   Initiate education regarding end-stage renal disease (ESRD) and dialysis, pending final diagnosis.

group_a.treatment_recommendations:
Clinical Summary: A female patient with a history of Roux-en-Y gastric bypass presents with acute, painless jaundice. Key findings include a cholestatic and hepatocellular injury pattern (AST 167, ALT 49, Alk Phos 167, Total/Direct Bilirubin 6.36/4.49), a palpable 3-cm firm epigastric nodule, hepatomegaly, and a history of an anastomotic ulcer with chronic iron deficiency anemia. The clinical presentation is highly suspicious for a malignant biliary or pancreatic obstruction.

Guideline Context: NCCN 2026 Oncology Guidelines

Treatment Plan:
**1. Immediate Diagnostic & Staging Workup:**
*   **Imaging:** Obtain high-quality cross-sectional imaging (contrast-enhanced CT of the chest/abdomen/pelvis or MRI/MRCP) to characterize the epigastric mass, define biliary anatomy, assess for vascular involvement, and evaluate for metastatic disease.
*   **Endoscopic Evaluation:** Proceed with ERCP for biliary decompression (stenting) and tissue acquisition via brushings or biopsy. Consider endoscopic ultrasound (EUS) with fine-needle aspiration (FNA) or biopsy of the mass for definitive histologic diagnosis.
*   **Tumor Markers:** Check serum CA 19-9, CEA, and AFP.
*   **Nutritional & Surgical Assessment:** Initiate nutritional consultation and obtain a surgical oncology evaluation to determine resectability potential.

**2. Multidisciplinary Tumor Board Review:** Present findings to a multidisciplinary team (medical oncology, surgical oncology, radiation oncology, gastroenterology, pathology, radiology) to confirm diagnosis and formulate a stage-directed treatment plan.

**3. Stage-Directed Therapy (Per NCCN Principles):**
*   **If Resectable (Pancreatic/Biliary Cancer):** Neoadjuvant therapy (multi-agent chemotherapy +/- chemoradiation) followed by restaging and potential resection, or upfront surgery followed by adjuvant chemotherapy based on final pathology.
*   **If Locally Advanced/Borderline Resectable:** Systemic therapy followed by re-evaluation for potential resection or consolidation with chemoradiation.
*   **If Metastatic:** Systemic therapy with combination chemotherapy as first-line treatment, with goals of controlling disease and managing symptoms. Consider germline and somatic molecular profiling to guide subsequent therapy.
*   **Supportive Care:** Continue management of biliary obstruction (stent patency), iron deficiency anemia, and nutritional support. Provide palliative care consultation early for symptom management and supportive oncology needs.

Supportive care for jaundice and anemia (e.g., iron transfusions, potential biliary drainage)

**Clinical Summary:**  
The patient is a woman with a complex past surgical history, including Roux-en-Y gastric bypass and laparoscopic cholecystectomy, who presents with acute, painless jaundice. Her clinical picture is notable for a mixed hepatocellular and cholestatic liver injury pattern, a palpable epigastric nodule, and a long-standing history of iron-deficiency anemia requiring transfusions. The absence of biliary dilation on initial ultrasound in the setting of significant hyperbilirubinemia raises concern for a malignant infiltrative or obstructive process. Subsequent diagnostic evaluation has revealed a diagnosis of metastatic non-small cell lung cancer, characterized by an EGFR L858R mutation and high PD-L1 expression (TPS 50%). The current presentation of jaundice is most consistent with metastatic biliary obstruction. The management plan appropriately centers on initiating systemic targeted therapy with osimertinib, with immune checkpoint inhibition as a potential future consideration based on her biomarker profile. group_b.treatment_recommendations:
**Clinical Summary:** The patient is a woman with a history of Roux-en-Y gastric bypass and chronic iron-deficiency anemia who presents with acute, painless jaundice. Evaluation revealed a mixed liver injury pattern and a palpable epigastric nodule. She has been diagnosed with metastatic non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC), with biomarker testing positive for an **EGFR L858R mutation** and **PD-L1 TPS 50%**. Her jaundice is attributed to metastatic biliary obstruction.

**Guideline Context:** NCCN 2026 Oncology Guidelines for Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer (specifically, Metastatic NSCLC with *EGFR* exon 19 deletion or exon 21 L858R mutation).

**Treatment Plan:**
1.  **First-Line Systemic Therapy:** Initiate **osimertinib** as the preferred first-line systemic therapy, in accordance with NCCN Category 1 recommendations for metastatic NSCLC harboring an *EGFR* exon 21 L858R mutation.
2.  **Management of Biliary Obstruction:** Urgently address the symptomatic biliary obstruction. Given the initial negative ultrasound, cross-sectional imaging (CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast or MRCP) is required to characterize the level and nature of the obstruction and to guide intervention (e.g., endoscopic, percutaneous, or surgical biliary drainage).
3.  **Supportive Care:**
    *   Continue management of iron-deficiency anemia with intravenous iron repletion as needed.
    *   Provide symptom management for jaundice-related pruritus if present.
    *   Monitor for and manage osimertinib-related adverse effects (e.g., diarrhea, rash, QT interval prolongation).
4.  **Future Therapeutic Considerations:** The high PD-L1 expression (TPS 50%) is noted. Per NCCN guidelines, immune checkpoint inhibitor monotherapy is a recommended first-line option for patients with high PD-L1 expression and no oncogenic driver. However, in the presence of a treatable *EGFR* mutation, targeted therapy (osimertinib) takes precedence. Immune checkpoint inhibitor therapy is generally not recommended concurrently with EGFR TKIs due to increased risk of toxicity. Its potential role would be re-evaluated upon progression on osimertinib, following biomarker re-assessment.

**Clinical Summary:**  
This is a female patient with a complex past medical history, including Roux-en-Y gastric bypass with subsequent chronic iron-deficiency anemia and anastomotic ulcer, who presents with acute, painless jaundice. Initial laboratory evaluation revealed a cholestatic and hepatocellular injury pattern, with a negative right upper quadrant ultrasound. The physical exam is notable for scleral icterus, abdominal distension, hepatomegaly, and a concerning firm, non-mobile, non-tender epigastric nodule. The clinical presentation, in the context of a significant family history of lung cancer, is highly suggestive of a malignant process causing biliary obstruction. Subsequent diagnostic workup has identified an underlying metastatic non-small cell lung cancer, characterized by an EGFR L858R mutation and high PD-L1 expression (TPS 50%). The primary management plan involves targeted therapy with osimertinib, with immune checkpoint inhibition as a potential additional therapeutic avenue given the high PD-L1 expression. Concurrent supportive management for her symptomatic jaundice and chronic iron-deficiency anemia is required. The patient has a documented allergy to Bactrim.
group_c.treatment_recommendations:
**Clinical Summary:** This is a female patient with a complex past medical history, including Roux-en-Y gastric bypass with subsequent chronic iron-deficiency anemia and anastomotic ulcer, who presents with acute, painless jaundice. Initial laboratory evaluation revealed a cholestatic and hepatocellular injury pattern, with a negative right upper quadrant ultrasound. The physical exam is notable for scleral icterus, abdominal distension, hepatomegaly, and a concerning firm, non-mobile, non-tender epigastric nodule. The clinical presentation, in the context of a significant family history of lung cancer, is highly suggestive of a malignant process causing biliary obstruction. Subsequent diagnostic workup has identified an underlying metastatic non-small cell lung cancer, characterized by an EGFR L858R mutation and high PD-L1 expression (TPS 50%). The primary management plan involves targeted therapy with osimertinib, with immune checkpoint inhibition as a potential additional therapeutic avenue given the high PD-L1 expression. Concurrent supportive management for her symptomatic jaundice and chronic iron-deficiency anemia is required. The patient has a documented allergy to Bactrim.

**Guideline Context:** NCCN 2026 Oncology Guidelines

**Treatment Plan:**
1.  **Primary Systemic Therapy:** Initiate osimertinib 80 mg orally once daily as first-line therapy for metastatic NSCLC with an EGFR exon 19 deletion or L858R mutation (NCCN Category 1 recommendation).
2.  **Integration of Immunotherapy:** Given the high PD-L1 expression (TPS 50%), the potential role of adding an immune checkpoint inhibitor (ICI) to osimertinib requires careful consideration. Per NCCN guidelines, combination EGFR TKI + ICI is **not recommended** due to increased risk of toxicity (specifically interstitial lung disease and hepatotoxicity) without clear efficacy benefit. Immunotherapy (e.g., pembrolizumab) is a preferred first-line option for metastatic NSCLC with high PD-L1 and *no* actionable driver mutations. Therefore, **osimertinib monotherapy is the guideline-concordant choice**.
3.  **Supportive & Symptomatic Management:**
    *   **Biliary Obstruction:** Urgent evaluation for biliary drainage (e.g., ERCP with stent placement or percutaneous transhepatic cholangiography) is indicated for symptomatic malignant obstruction. This should be coordinated with Gastroenterology/Interventional Radiology.
    *   **Anemia:** Continue management of iron-deficiency anemia with intravenous iron supplementation as needed.
    *   **Allergy:** Document and maintain allergy alert for Bactrim (trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole).
4.  **Monitoring & Follow-up:**
    *   Assess for treatment response and toxicity with clinical evaluation and CT imaging of chest/abdomen/pelvis in 2-3 months.
    *   Monitor liver function tests closely, particularly during the initial phase of osimertinib therapy and after any biliary intervention.
    *   Patient education on potential side effects of osimertinib (e.g., diarrhea, rash, paronychia, ILD symptoms).

Name: ___ Unit No: ___ Admission Date: ___ Discharge Date: ___ Date of Birth: ___ Sex: M Service: NEUROLOGY Allergies: Penicillins / Ativan Attending: ___ Chief Complaint: R eyelid droop and pain Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure: Lumbar puncture with fluoroscopic guidance ___ History of Present Illness: Mr. ___ is a ___ year old man with a complicated past medical history which includes liver transplant in ___ secondary to alcohol and hepatitis C cirrhosis, end-stage renal disease on HD ___ to immunosuppressive agents s/p liver transplant (currently being worked up for kidney transplant), hypertension, diabetes mellitus type 2 (not on home insulin), tonsillar cancer status post resection ___ years ago and radiation therapy to the left side of his neck. He presents today for evaluation of new onset right eye ptosis. History obtained by patient as well as patient's wife and daughter who are at bedside. The patient reports that he was in his usual state of health up until a few weeks ago when, during an elective hospital admission at ___ as part of his kidney transplant workup, he developed left supraorbital eye pain associated with left eye ptosis with progression to left CN3 palsy with limited adduction, upgaze, and downgaze. Workup at the time included CT/CTA Head and Neck with and without contrast and MRI/MRA without contrast, which demonstrated by report only (imaging not yet obtained) no intracranial abnormality. The remainder of his neurological exam at the time was notable for He was diagnosed with a diabetic CN3 palsy at the time. After his kidney transplant workup was complete, he was discharged home with a plan for routine follow-up with Neuro-opthalmology. When he arrived back to the ___ area, he reported being tired. His left eye remained largely closed without significant improvement in symptoms. Three days ago, he noticed that the initial supraorbital pain that was on his left eye seemed to have traversed to his right orbit with similar supraorbital pressure. Yesterday his right eye became droopy so he presented for urgent eval out of concern that his right eye will also suffer from a nerve paralysis. Past Medical History: #EtOH/HCV cirrhosis complicated by ___ s/p liver transplant in ___. 1) Complications: -S/p tx w/Harvoni in ___ with sustained virologic response -De ___ ascites without cirrhosis or clear lymphatic or venous obstruction -ESRD on HD MWF, transplant workup underway #HTN #Tonsillar CA s/p XRT ___ #IDDM (___) #OSA (home CPAP) Social History: ___ Family History: No known neurologic diseases. No known history of cancer or auto-immune conditions. Physical Exam: ADMISSION EXAM: Vitals: 36.7, HR50, RR18, BP140/65, SaO2 98%. Gener

**Extracted Data:**  
```json
{
  "diagnosis": "Progressive bilateral cranial nerve III (CN3) palsy, initially presenting as left-sided diabetic cranial nerve III palsy with new right-sided involvement.",
  "biomarkers": ["History of diabetes mellitus type 2", "History of hepatitis C (sustained virologic response post-treatment)", "End-stage renal disease on hemodialysis"],
  "treatments": ["Neurology/Neuro-ophthalmology follow-up", "Immunosuppressive therapy (post-liver transplant)", "Hemodialysis (MWF)", "Home CPAP for OSA"]
}
```

**Clinical Summary:**  
The patient is a man with a complex medical history including liver transplant (with ongoing immunosuppression), end-stage renal disease on hemodialysis, type 2 diabetes mellitus, and prior tonsillar cancer status post radiation. He presents with a progressive, painful bilateral oculomotor (CN3) palsy. The condition began several weeks ago with left supraorbital pain and ptosis, progressing to a complete left CN3 palsy, which was initially diagnosed as a diabetic mononeuropathy after a negative CT/CTA and MRI/MRA of the head/neck. The left-sided symptoms have persisted without significant improvement. He now reports migration of the characteristic supraorbital pain to the right orbit, followed by the acute onset of right eyelid ptosis, prompting urgent evaluation. The development of sequential, painful bilateral cranial neuropathies in an immunocompromised host raises significant concern for an alternative, more systemic etiology beyond an isolated microvascular diabetic palsy, necessitating a comprehensive diagnostic reassessment.
group_a.treatment_recommendations:
**Clinical Summary:** The patient is a man with a complex medical history including liver transplant (with ongoing immunosuppression), end-stage renal disease on hemodialysis, type 2 diabetes mellitus, and prior tonsillar cancer status post radiation. He presents with a progressive, painful bilateral oculomotor (CN3) palsy. The condition began several weeks ago with left supraorbital pain and ptosis, progressing to a complete left CN3 palsy, which was initially diagnosed as a diabetic mononeuropathy after a negative CT/CTA and MRI/MRA of the head/neck. The left-sided symptoms have persisted without significant improvement. He now reports migration of the characteristic supraorbital pain to the right orbit, followed by the acute onset of right eyelid ptosis, prompting urgent evaluation. The development of sequential, painful bilateral cranial neuropathies in an immunocompromised host raises significant concern for an alternative, more systemic etiology beyond an isolated microvascular diabetic palsy, necessitating a comprehensive diagnostic reassessment.

**Guideline Context:** NCCN 2026 Oncology Guidelines
**Treatment Plan:**
Given the patient's history of prior tonsillar cancer (s/p resection and radiation) and his immunocompromised status post-transplant, the development of new, progressive, painful bilateral cranial neuropathies is a **neurologic emergency** concerning for neoplastic or infectious/inflammatory complications. The initial workup was insufficient for this clinical scenario.

1.  **Immediate Diagnostic Re-evaluation:**
    *   **Neuroimaging:** Urgent **MRI Brain and Orbits with and without contrast**, with specific attention to the cavernous sinus, orbital apex, and cranial nerve pathways. The prior non-contrast MRA was inadequate. Consider MR venography to evaluate for cavernous sinus thrombosis.
    *   **Lumbar Puncture (LP):** Cerebrospinal fluid analysis for cell count, differential, protein, glucose, cytology, flow cytometry (to assess for lymphomatous involvement), and infectious studies (including VZV, HSV, CMV, EBV, fungal, and bacterial cultures/PCR as indicated by immune status).
    *   **Infectious/Inflammatory Workup:** Given immunosuppression, evaluate for opportunistic infections (e.g., VZV, CMV, fungal) and inflammatory conditions (e.g., sarcoidosis, IgG4-related disease). Check serum ACE, IgG4 levels, and infectious serologies.
    *   **Oncologic Restaging:** Given history of tonsillar cancer, consider PET-CT to evaluate for locoregional recurrence or distant metastatic disease. Biopsy of any accessible lesion identified on imaging should be pursued.

2.  **Multidisciplinary Consultation:**
    *   **Neurology/Neuro-ophthalmology:** For definitive localization and management of the cranial neuropathies.
    *   **Neuro-oncology & Medical Oncology:** To guide the diagnostic workup for potential neoplastic (metastatic, lymphomatous, or perineural spread from prior cancer) causes.
    *   **Infectious Disease:** For management of potential CNS infection in an immunocompromised host.
    *   **Transplant Team:** To coordinate care and review/optimize immunosuppressive regimen in the context of a potential new serious diagnosis.

3.  **Supportive & Symptomatic Care:**
    *   **Pain Management:** Manage neuropathic orbital pain with appropriate analgesics.
    *   **Ophthalmologic Care:** Artificial tears and eye patching as needed to protect the cornea from exposure keratopathy due to ptosis and impaired eye closure.
    *   **Continue** management of comorbidities: hemodialysis, immunosuppression, diabetes, hypertension.

4.  **Definitive Treatment:**
    *   **Will be entirely guided by the results of the above workup.** Options may include:
        *   **Antiviral/Antimicrobial Therapy:** If an infectious etiology is confirmed.
        *   **Immunosuppression/Corticosteroids:** If an inflammatory (e.g., sarcoid, IgG4-RD) or immune-mediated process is diagnosed, in coordination with the transplant team.
        *   **Oncologic Therapy:** If a neoplastic cause is identified (recurrent squamous cell carcinoma, lymphoma, metastasis), treatment will follow **NCCN Guidelines** for the specific malignancy and may involve radiation therapy, systemic therapy, or a combination.
        *   **Surgical Intervention:** For biopsy or resection if a discrete, accessible lesion is identified.

**Next Steps:** Admit for urgent diagnostic workup and multidisciplinary management. The priority is to rule out life-threatening or vision-threatening conditions such as skull base metastasis, lymphoma, CNS infection, or cavernous sinus syndrome.

structured_data.diagnosis: Progressive bilateral cranial nerve III (CN3) palsy with right eye involvement, in a patient with history of liver transplant, ESRD, diabetes mellitus type 2, and tonsillar cancer status post radiation. Previously diagnosed with diabetic CN3 palsy, but progression to contralateral side suggests alternative or contributing etiology. MedSAM tool confirms Progressive Disease (PD) with lesion area increased by 45%.
structured_data.biomarkers: No actionable solid tumor biomarkers identified (Pathology AI). No actionable genomic targets found (OncoKB).
structured_data.treatments: Urgent neurological and neuro-ophthalmological evaluation. Consider repeat neuroimaging (MRI/MRA with contrast) given progression and bilateral symptoms. Manage underlying conditions: immunosuppression per transplant protocol, diabetes, hypertension. Symptomatic management of ptosis and pain. Rule out compressive, inflammatory, or infiltrative causes given cancer history and immunosuppressed state.

**Clinical Summary:**  
This is a complex patient with a history of liver transplant (on immunosuppression), end-stage renal disease on hemodialysis, type 2 diabetes mellitus, and prior tonsillar cancer status post radiation, who presents with progressive bilateral cranial nerve III palsies. Initially, he was diagnosed with a left-sided diabetic mononeuropathy during a recent hospitalization, supported by neuroimaging reportedly showing no acute intracranial process. However, the subsequent development of identical right supraorbital pain and ptosis, with the left-sided symptoms showing no improvement, significantly alters the clinical picture. The bilateral and progressive nature of the deficits, in the context of his immunosuppressed state and prior head/neck radiation, raises strong concern for an alternative, unifying etiology beyond microvascular disease. This could include an infiltrative, inflammatory, compressive, or neoplastic process affecting the cavernous sinus or posterior communicating artery region. AI-assisted imaging analysis (MedSAM) indicates a 45% increase in lesion area, formally classifying this as progressive disease. While comprehensive biomarker and genomic profiling (Pathology AI, OncoKB) did not reveal actionable solid tumor targets, this does not exclude a non-metastatic local process or other pathology. Management requires urgent, advanced neuroimaging with contrast to reassess the cranial nerve pathways, alongside coordinated evaluation by neurology and neuro-ophthalmology to guide targeted diagnostic testing and treatment, while continuing supportive management of his multiple chronic conditions.
group_b.treatment_recommendations:
Clinical Summary: A patient with a history of liver transplant (on immunosuppression), ESRD on HD, type 2 DM, and prior tonsillar cancer s/p radiation presents with progressive bilateral cranial nerve III palsies. Initially diagnosed with left diabetic mononeuropathy, he has since developed identical right-sided symptoms without left-sided improvement. AI imaging analysis (MedSAM) indicates a 45% increase in lesion area, classifying this as Progressive Disease (PD). Comprehensive biomarker/genomic profiling (Pathology AI, OncoKB) revealed no actionable solid tumor targets. The bilateral, progressive deficits in an immunosuppressed patient with prior radiation raise concern for an alternative unifying etiology (e.g., infiltrative, inflammatory, compressive, or neoplastic process).

Guideline Context: NCCN 2026 Oncology Guidelines

Treatment Plan:
1.  **Urgent Diagnostic Re-evaluation:**
    *   **Imaging:** Obtain an urgent MRI/MRA of the brain and orbits **with and without contrast**, with specific attention to the cavernous sinus, posterior communicating arteries, and cranial nerve pathways. Compare directly to prior studies.
    *   **Consultation:** Urgent formal evaluations by **Neurology** and **Neuro-Ophthalmology**.
    *   **Additional Testing:** Based on imaging findings and specialist input, consider:
        *   Lumbar puncture for CSF analysis (cell count, cytology, flow cytometry, IgG index, oligoclonal bands) to evaluate for inflammatory/infectious/infiltrative processes.
        *   Inflammatory/paraneoplastic serologies (e.g., ANA, ANCA, anti-GQ1b, anti-Hu, anti-Ri).
        *   Given history of tonsillar cancer, consider PET-CT if there is concern for locoregional recurrence or metastatic disease.

2.  **Therapeutic Management:**
    *   **Etiology-Directed Therapy:** Initiate treatment based on the identified etiology.
        *   If **inflammatory** (e.g., granulomatous, IgG4-related): initiate high-dose corticosteroids with a slow taper, with close monitoring of diabetes and immunosuppression levels.
        *   If **neoplastic/compressive**: Refer to Neurosurgery and/or Radiation Oncology for consideration of biopsy, resection, or radiation therapy per relevant NCCN CNS or Head and Neck Cancer guidelines.
        *   If **infectious**: Initiate appropriate antimicrobial therapy.
        *   If no alternative etiology is found and **diabetic microvascular disease** remains a contributing factor, optimize glycemic control.
    *   **Supportive & Symptomatic Care:**
        *   Manage ptosis (e.g., eyelid crutches, taping) to prevent amblyopia and improve function.
        *   Manage neuropathic pain per guideline-based approaches.
        *   Continue management of chronic conditions (ESRD/HD, transplant immunosuppression, HTN, DM) in coordination with relevant specialists.

3.  **Monitoring & Follow-up:**
    *   Close clinical monitoring for progression of cranial neuropathies or development of new neurological symptoms.
    *   Repeat neuroimaging in 4-8 weeks to assess response to any initiated therapy or for further progression.
    *   Multidisciplinary tumor board review is recommended if a neoplastic process is confirmed or highly suspected.

**Clinical Summary:**
This is a patient with a complex medical history, including status post liver transplant (on immunosuppression), end-stage renal disease on hemodialysis, diabetes mellitus type 2, and a history of tonsillar cancer with neck radiation, who presents with a progressive, painful bilateral third cranial nerve (CN3) palsy. Initially presenting with a left-sided CN3 palsy attributed to diabetic mononeuropathy, the subsequent development of identical right supraorbital pain and ptosis over a period of weeks renders the initial diagnosis highly suspect. The bilateral and sequential nature of the deficit, in the context of immunosuppression and prior head/neck malignancy, raises significant concern for a central or compressive etiology, such as an infiltrative, inflammatory, or neoplastic process at the cavernous sinus or posterior communicating artery. AI-assisted imaging analysis (MedSAM) suggests significant disease progression. Current biomarker analyses (Pathology AI, OncoKB) do not reveal actionable oncogenic targets for common solid tumors. The management plan must prioritize urgent, advanced neuroimaging with contrast to identify a structural cause, coordinated care with transplant and nephrology services for comorbidity management, and strict avoidance of documented medication allergies.
group_c.treatment_recommendations:
**Clinical Summary:** This is a patient with a complex medical history, including status post liver transplant (on immunosuppression), end-stage renal disease on hemodialysis, diabetes mellitus type 2, and a history of tonsillar cancer with neck radiation, who presents with a progressive, painful bilateral third cranial nerve (CN3) palsy. Initially presenting with a left-sided CN3 palsy attributed to diabetic mononeuropathy, the subsequent development of identical right supraorbital pain and ptosis over a period of weeks renders the initial diagnosis highly suspect. The bilateral and sequential nature of the deficit, in the context of immunosuppression and prior head/neck malignancy, raises significant concern for a central or compressive etiology, such as an infiltrative, inflammatory, or neoplastic process at the cavernous sinus or posterior communicating artery. AI-assisted imaging analysis (MedSAM) suggests significant disease progression. Current biomarker analyses (Pathology AI, OncoKB) do not reveal actionable oncogenic targets for common solid tumors. The management plan must prioritize urgent, advanced neuroimaging with contrast to identify a structural cause, coordinated care with transplant and nephrology services for comorbidity management, and strict avoidance of documented medication allergies.

**Guideline Context:** NCCN 2026 Oncology Guidelines

**Treatment Plan:**
1.  **Urgent Diagnostic Re-evaluation:**
    *   **Imaging:** Obtain and personally review prior CT/CTA and MRI/MRA. Immediately schedule and perform a **contrast-enhanced MRI of the brain and orbits with dedicated cavernous sinus views and MRA of the circle of Willis**. Given the history of tonsillar cancer and neck radiation, include imaging of the skull base and nasopharynx to evaluate for local recurrence or radiation-induced malignancy.
    *   **Biopsy Consideration:** If imaging identifies a discrete mass or infiltrative lesion, consult Neurosurgery and/or Interventional Neuroradiology for consideration of biopsy to obtain a definitive histopathological diagnosis, guided by the safest accessible approach.
    *   **Lumbar Puncture:** Consider repeat lumbar puncture with cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) analysis for cytology, flow cytometry (if hematologic malignancy suspected), and infectious/inflammatory markers, given the immunosuppressed state.

2.  **Multidisciplinary Oncology Evaluation:**
    *   Convene a tumor board or multidisciplinary consultation involving **Neuro-oncology, Radiation Oncology, Medical Oncology, and the patient's Transplant Hepatology and Nephrology teams**.
    *   **Differential Diagnosis & Workup:** Prioritize ruling out:
        *   **Metastatic disease** from prior tonsillar cancer or a new primary.
        *   **Lymphoproliferative disorder** (e.g., CNS lymphoma) related to chronic immunosuppression.
        *   **Meningeal carcinomatosis.**
        *   **Skull base/local recurrence** from prior radiation field.
    *   **Repeat Biomarker Analysis:** If a biopsy is performed, submit tissue for comprehensive molecular profiling (including next-generation sequencing) to identify potential therapeutic targets, as prior OncoKB analysis was negative for the "current indication" (which may have been undefined).

3.  **Comorbidity Management & Supportive Care:**
    *   **Immunosuppression:** Collaborate with the transplant team to review and potentially modify immunosuppressive regimens, balancing rejection risk with the need to investigate/ treat a potential neoplastic or inflammatory process.
    *   **Renal/Dialysis Coordination:** All imaging and potential systemic therapies must be planned in coordination with Nephrology, considering contrast dye protocols for ESRD and drug clearance.
    *   **Symptom Management:** Provide appropriate analgesia for orbital pain. Ophthalmology consultation for ptosis management (e.g., eyelid crutches) to prevent amblyopia and improve function.
    *   **Allergy Alert:** Document and enforce strict avoidance of Penicillins and Ativan (Lorazepam) across all care settings.

4.  **Treatment Strategy (Pending Diagnosis):**
    *   **If a neoplasm is confirmed,** treatment will be guided by the specific histology and molecular profile per **NCCN Guidelines** for that malignancy, with adaptations for his immunosuppressed status and ESRD. Options may include:
        *   **Focal Radiation Therapy:** Consider for a localized lesion, with careful planning due to prior neck radiation.
        *   **Systemic Therapy:** Selection will depend on histology, renal dosing requirements, and drug interactions with immunosuppressants.
        *   **Reduction of Immunosuppression:** If post-transplant lymphoproliferative disorder (PTLD) is diagnosed.
    *   **If an inflammatory/infectious etiology is identified,** tailor antimicrobial or immunomodulatory therapy accordingly.
